FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly U.S. ABCD Study (21-site longitudinal cohort, ages 9-10 at baseline, ABCD Data Release 5.0, NDA). Methods: "The ABCD Study is a 21-site 10-year longitudinal study with 11,880 participants at baseline (ages 9-10), funded by the National Institutes of Health."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD EARS subsample (n=1,463 with usable ABCD-EARS data). Reports intraclass correlations, Pearson correlations, beta regressions, t-tests, and Bland-Altman plots examining validity and variability of passively-sensed smartphone keyboard and app-use measures and differences by sex and OS.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Scientific Reports (2024); no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables are present (passive app use, keystrokes, social apps = texting + video chatting + social media, self-reported smartphone use). However, the other side of every analysis is exclusively demographic or contextual: biological sex (t-tests: females > males, t=6.9, p<0.001), race/ethnicity, household income, parental education, operating system. No individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, sleep, substance use, academic) appears on the other side of any analysis. The binding other-side rule requires the non-SM side to be individual-trait-level; demographic-only on the other side yields NOT_MET. The paper is a descriptive/validation study of EARS measures with sociodemographic group comparisons only.

notes: Validation/descriptive paper for ABCD-EARS passive smartphone sensing. All inferential analyses pair smartphone use variables with demographic predictors only (sex, income, parental education, race/ethnicity, OS). Fails IC2 other-side rule.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper is explicitly about the U.S. ABCD Study (11,876 children, ages 9-10, 21 data collection sites, NIH-funded). Introduction: "The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study is the largest and most comprehensive long-term study on brain development and child health in the United States."

FT_IC5.evidence: Paper introduces NowIKnowMyABCD, a community resource hub (GitHub discussion board + website) for ABCD researchers. No empirical analysis of ABCD data is reported. This is a resource/tool description paper equivalent to a study protocol or resource-availability paper. No statistical analyses, no outcomes, no participants analyzed.

notes: Resource/tool paper describing a community platform (website + discussion board) for ABCD researchers. No empirical data analysis conducted. Exclusion code FT-EC3.

FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly ABCD Study: "N = 11,868 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study followed from ages ~9/10 to ~13/14." ABCD data DOI 10.15154/z563-zd24 cited.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multilevel logistic regression, descriptive statistics, intraclass correlation coefficients, and Spearman correlations on ABCD data across 5 waves (ages 9/10 to 13/14).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024); no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables are present as DVs: social media hrs/day, texting hrs/day, video chat hrs/day are qualifying SM/phone variables. Table 2 reports ORs for predictors of >2 hrs/day use. However, all predictors (the other side) are exclusively demographic: biological sex (female vs. male OR=2.41, 95% CI: 2.21-2.62 for social media), parent education, combined parent income, parent marital status. Study wave (time) is contextual. No individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, sleep, substance use, academic, personality, etc.) appears on the other side of any analysis anywhere in the paper (main, supplementary, or sensitivity). The binding other-side rule requires the non-SM side to be individual-trait-level; demographic-only on the other side yields NOT_MET.

notes: Descriptive epidemiology of technology subtypes (TV, social media, texting, video chat, video games, YouTube) mapped against sociodemographic covariates only. All analyses pair technology use with demographic predictors (sex, parental education, income, marital status). Fails IC2 other-side rule. Exclusion code FT-EC2.
